─[ c8 Subprocess Coverage Blind Spot ]─ ┐

name: c8-subprocess-coverage-blind-spot description: c8 cannot instrument code running in child processes — subprocess-tested modules show 0% coverage despite being exercised user-invocable: false allowed-tools: Read, Bash

c8 Subprocess Coverage Blind Spot

c8 (and Istanbul/nyc) instruments code loaded in the current Node.js process only. Any code executed via execSync, spawn, or fork runs in a separate process where c8 has no visibility, so coverage for that code reports as 0%.

Snags

Symptom: Coverage report shows suspiciously low numbers (e.g. 61%) on a module that integration tests clearly exercise. The tests pass, but coverage doesn’t reflect it.

Root cause: The tests invoke the module through a subprocess rather than importing it directly.

// Bad — c8 cannot see lib/banner.js execute here
execSync('node hooks/session-init.js');

// Good — c8 instruments the module in-process
const { buildBanner, scanWork } = require('../../lib/banner');
buildBanner();
scanWork();

Fix: Add direct unit tests that require() the module and call its exported functions in-process. Keep subprocess-based integration tests for end-to-end validation — they confirm wiring — but they cannot substitute for unit tests when coverage is the goal.

Detection checklist:

  • Coverage is low on a module you know is tested
  • Integration tests for that module all pass
  • Test invocations use execSync, spawn, or fork

Resolution Pattern

  1. Identify the module with low coverage.
  2. Find the test(s) driving it — confirm they use subprocess invocation.
  3. Add a unit test file that imports the module directly and calls each exported function.
  4. Re-run coverage — the number should jump to reflect what’s actually exercised in-process.
PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
